it would be useful to capture state of things that are going on in other instances of claude code simulatenaously that might affect the current work being done -- consult with an opus agent -- how could we design this?

what I have in mind is that we would introduce into the context of an agent for example if the agent finds some problem in the code and there's another agent working at the same time we could inject some awareness -- or it could be as simple as, when an agent is working on something we keep some kind of awanreess that that project base (meaning, that whether it happens on worktree A or worktree B, its the same git base so they all share the same awareness) -- we would keep like a brief "Agent on branch X: Fixing authentication bug where OAuth doesn't perist credentials[10s ago]\nAgent on branch Y: Cleaning up dead code [2m ago]"

1 hour after the agent is done we would clear it (or keep it in a file but not inject into the agent's system)

something like that....
Designed a multi-agent awareness system with per-agent files keyed by git-common-dir, decided against LLM relevance gating, and proposed tracking with PostToolUse heartbeats for liveness.

the goal is not to avoid agents tripping over each other's file edits; the goal is to under when an agent is working on something (particularly if relevant to the other agent!) -- imagine if you are working in an open office, employees are able to know, in some way, what their proximate teammates are working on, that's kinda what a standup is all about, or a slack watercooler or whatever -- this is important so agents can find their boundaries on the work they need to do ("I was fixing bug A but found these other 16 bugs so I also worked on those" x repeated 16 times, on per agent that found all 16 bugs)

which also means that the user's last or most relevant message is not enough to determine what the agent will work on.
Revised the design to track both initial tasks and touched files via PostToolUse, confirming PostToolUse is essential since the opening prompt alone doesn't reflect what an agent actually explores.

you are thinking way too narrowly -- files is a terrible heuristic that will be riddled with hallucination because the system has to do so much guessing on why the file is being
  changed. Unless what you're thinking is using the tooluse as a hook to review the transcript to see what the agent is actualy working on based on its own thinking and re-categorize
  what the agent is working on every x minutes of active work or soething like that.
Asked what the user would like to clarify about the design.

I think we do it after backing-off windows of continous tool-use without interruptions, meaning, we don't want to fire-off because the agent used 5 or 10 tools to read some files because the user asked how something is working, so I would say let's wait first 1 minute of continous work, and then back-off to 2.5 minutes, 5 minutes and every 10 minutes thereafter. If the user interrupts or the agent ends we reset it back to 1 minute.

We should use a specific model for this that's fast and has a good context window, let's configure by default 4o-mini.

Locked the distill trigger as a backoff schedule (1→2.5→5→10→10 min) over uninterrupted work streaks with resets on UserPromptSubmit/Stop, defaulting to 4o-mini, and proposed writing a spec doc before implementation.
be honest: where did you see the "spec-first-development-workflow wiki"?

Completed the spec at `docs/product-spec/agent-awareness.md`, fixing two bugs the advisor caught (concurrent write collision and unconditional Stop-distill), and listed five open questions.

Confirmed PostToolUse can inject system reminders, agreed SQLite is strictly better (eliminates the concurrent-write bug and enables delta queries), and posed five design questions about durability, change types, cursors, throttling, and scope.

Committed as `1183f81`, noting the full feature is split across two commits due to another agent sweeping shared-file changes.

Committed as two scoped commits: `078f78a` for the viewer and `41d70cb` for the rename, flagging potential commit collisions from concurrent agents.
